metadata.summary: Framework for PM tool evaluation using weighted scoring, PoC protocol, TCO analysis, and ADR templates. Covers Jira, Linear, ClickUp, GitHub Projects.

content.title: PM Tools Comparison
content.summary: A structured framework for evaluating and selecting project management tools through a weighted scoring matrix (Core Features 30%, Usability 25%, Integrations 20%, Enterprise 15%, Cost 10%), a two-week proof-of-concept protocol, total cost of ownership (TCO) analysis, and an ADR template for recording the decision. Covers Jira, Linear, ClickUp, GitHub Projects, GitLab Boards, Azure DevOps, Notion, Trello, and Asana.

why.p: PM tool evaluations fail from anchor bias (the evaluator's preferred tool scores highest) and TCO underestimation (migration + custom reporting routinely doubles the projected cost). A pre-defined weighted matrix with measured PoC data — not vendor marketing — and a forced-rank step prevents score inflation and produces a defensible, auditable decision.

list.item: Selecting an initial PM tool for a new team or product.
Re-evaluating an existing tool when pain points (speed, cost, integrations) accumulate.
Building an ADR for a board or leadership review with quantitative scoring.
Mid-migration sanity check: confirm the chosen tool actually scores higher on what mattered.
Single-person projects — overhead exceeds gain; use whatever is already available.
When the org has a mandated tool (e.g., Jira via enterprise contract) and switching is not on the table.
Hotfix or firefighting periods — tool selection should not be the response to delivery problems.

rule.statement: Define the top 3 must-have requirements BEFORE looking at any tool; weights must be fixed before scoring begins.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc weighting is the primary source of anchor bias — teams unconsciously adjust weights to confirm the tool they already prefer.

list.item: Core Features (30%): issue tracking, board views, sprint management
Usability (25%): learning curve, performance, mobile app
Integrations (20%): GitHub/GitLab, Slack, CI/CD
Enterprise (15%): SSO/SAML, permissions model, audit logs
Cost (10%): price per user, free tier quality

section.name: reference-scores
section.p: Scores are research-derived baselines; replace with PoC-measured values before final decision. Stale evidence (older than 12 months) must be flagged.

list.item: Core Features: issue tracking 10, board views 9, sprint management 10
Usability: learning curve 5, performance 6, mobile 7
Integrations: GitHub 9, Slack 8, CI/CD 9
Enterprise: SSO 10, permissions 10, audit logs 10
Cost: price/user 6, free tier 7 — Reference total: 7.5/10
Core Features: issue tracking 9, board views 9, sprint management 9
Usability: learning curve 9, performance 10, mobile 8
Integrations: GitHub 10, Slack 9, CI/CD 8
Enterprise: SSO 9, permissions 8, audit logs 8
Cost: price/user 7, free tier 7 — Reference total: 8.5/10
Core Features: issue tracking 9, board views 10, sprint management 8
Usability: learning curve 6, performance 7, mobile 7
Integrations: GitHub 7, Slack 8, CI/CD 6
Enterprise: SSO 8, permissions 8, audit logs 7
Cost: price/user 8, free tier 8 — Reference total: 7.5/10
Core Features: issue tracking 8, board views 7, sprint management 6
Usability: learning curve 7, performance 8, mobile 6
Integrations: GitHub 10, Slack 7, CI/CD 10
Enterprise: SSO 9, permissions 9, audit logs 9
Cost: price/user 8, free tier 8 — Reference total: 7.8/10
Core Features: issue tracking 7, board views 8, sprint management 6
Usability: learning curve 7, performance 7, mobile 8
Integrations: GitHub 6, Slack 7, CI/CD 4
Enterprise: SSO 8, permissions 7, audit logs 6
Cost: price/user 9, free tier 9 — Reference total: 7.0/10

rule.statement: Cap evaluated tools at 4-5; always include an "incumbent + zero-change" baseline as one of the options.
rule.rationale: More than 5 tools produces analysis paralysis; the incumbent baseline prevents the evaluation from systematically undervaluing switching costs.

rule.statement: Run a forced-rank step after scoring (rank A > B > C, no ties allowed) before writing the recommendation.
rule.rationale: Score totals frequently cluster within margin-of-error; forced ranking surfaces the actual preference without false precision.

antipattern.description: Scoring from vendor marketing rather than PoC measurements.
antipattern.reason: Vendor claims systematically overstate usability and integration quality; only measured data from your team's actual workflows is decision-grade.

rule.statement: Score from team-measured PoC data, not vendor marketing; require a vendor-docs URL for every claimed integration.
rule.rationale: Vendor claims systematically overstate usability; agents hallucinate integrations that do not exist. Only measured data is decision-grade.

list.item: Duration: 2 weeks minimum (scaling pain only surfaces at >10k issues; note this limitation)
Participants: 1 PM representative, 2 developer representatives, 1 manager representative
Test scenarios: create project structure, import sample data, setup sprint, configure automations, test integrations, generate reports
Measured outputs: setup time (hours), daily workflow time (minutes), feature coverage (%), team satisfaction (1-10), performance (1-10)

section.name: tco-structure
section.p: Total cost of ownership must include three categories; migration and custom-reporting costs almost always exceed projections.

list.item: Direct costs: license per user × user count × 12 months, add-ons, support tier
Indirect costs: implementation (setup hours × hourly rate), migration (consultant days × day rate), training (sessions × cost), ongoing admin (hours/month × rate × 12)
Hidden costs: productivity loss during transition, integration development, custom reporting, abandoned workflows

rule.statement: Capture migration cost at item-count granularity (#issues, #automations, #integrations) before signing; line-item estimation prevents systematic undercount.
rule.rationale: High-level "migration cost" estimates routinely miss custom automation conversion and historical data queries; item-count forces the evaluator to enumerate what must move.

section.name: decision-matrix
section.p: Five weighted categories for final tool selection. Weights below are defaults; adjust per team before evaluation begins.

list.item: Technical fit (30%): feature coverage, integration capabilities, API quality, performance
Usability (25%): learning curve, daily efficiency, mobile experience, documentation quality
Strategic fit (20%): vendor stability, roadmap alignment, community ecosystem, scalability
Cost (15%): license cost, implementation cost, ongoing admin, 3-year TCO
Risk (10%): migration complexity, vendor lock-in, data portability, support quality

antipattern.description: Agent favors newer or more frequently mentioned online tools.
antipattern.reason: Recency bias from training data; require PoC measurements from the actual team, not sentiment from internet discussions.

antipattern.description: Agent runs comparison and recommendation in one prompt.
antipattern.reason: Produces anchored outputs; separate steps: (1) fill matrix, (2) forced rank, (3) draft recommendation, (4) human review.

antipattern.description: Pricing data scraped from vendor sites without checking recency.
antipattern.reason: Pricing changes frequently; require explicit date-checked URLs and mark cells older than 12 months as STALE.

example.title: PM Tool Decision: Jira to Linear
example.note: Engineering team of 30; primary pain points are Jira performance and complex UX.
example.code:
# ADR-005: PM Tool Selection — Jira to Linear
## Status: Accepted
## Context Current tool (Jira) causes productivity issues: 6-second average page load, high learning curve for new engineers, and complex permission model requiring dedicated admin time. Team of 30 engineers needs a faster, simpler alternative.
## Decision Migrate to Linear as primary PM tool.
## Evaluation Summary | Tool | Score | Key Finding | |----------------|-------|-------------| | Linear | 8.5 | Best UX; fastest tool in PoC (avg 0.3s load) | | GitHub Projects| 7.8 | Good code integration; limited sprint features | | ClickUp | 7.5 | Most features; performance concern at scale | | Notion | 7.0 | Better for docs than PM tracking | | Jira (baseline)| 6.8 | Current state; migration cost set to zero |
## Key Factors 1. Speed: Linear 0.3s vs Jira 6s average load in PoC 2. UX: keyboard-first design matches developer workflow 3. GitHub: native PR link to issue (no plugin required) 4. Price: $10/user comparable to Jira at this scale
## Consequences Positive: improved daily velocity, better developer experience, 40% reduction in admin overhead. Negative: migration effort, some Jira automation features unavailable, 2-week learning curve.
## Migration Plan Phase 1: Pilot with Platform team (2 weeks) Phase 2: Migrate remaining teams (4 weeks) Phase 3: Jira read-only, decommission in 30 days
## Review Date: 6 months post-migration

example.title: PM Tool Comparison Recommendation
example.note: One-page summary for steering committee after PoC completion.
example.code:
# PM Tool Comparison — Recommendation Summary
## Option 1: Linear (Recommended) — Score 8.5/10 Price: $10/user/month — 3-year TCO: ~$45,000 Pros: fastest UX in PoC, best GitHub integration, modern opinionated design Cons: less customizable, smaller ecosystem, newer vendor
## Option 2: GitHub Projects — Score 7.8/10 Price: included in GitHub plan — 3-year TCO: ~$0 incremental Pros: zero migration, native code+issue integration, no vendor lock-in Cons: limited sprint management, no agile reporting, PM overhead high
## Option 3: ClickUp — Score 7.5/10 Price: $12/user/month — 3-year TCO: ~$52,000 Pros: most features, highly customizable, active roadmap Cons: overwhelms new users, performance at scale unproven, complex permissions
## Recommendation Linear: best fit for an engineering-led team that values speed and code integration over customization breadth. PoC measured 20x faster load time vs. current Jira. Pre-commit to a 6-month re-evaluation with clear rollback criteria.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use a Claude subagent to fill the scoring matrix from public data + the team's stated requirements, then render a draft ADR. Humans run the live PoC and only feed the agent measured scores (setup time, daily-flow time, satisfaction). Keep the agent away from the final recommendation — it's a synthesis assistant, not a decision-maker.

list.item: faion-pm-agent — produces the comparison matrix + ADR draft from research.
General-purpose Claude subagent — runs the WebSearch sweep on each tool's pricing, integrations, recent changes.
faion-sdd-executor-agent — once a tool is chosen, generates the migration task tree.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.p: For tools A, B, C, fill the scoring matrix. Score each cell 1-10 with one citation (URL or vendor doc). Mark any cell where evidence is older than 12 months as STALE.

Given the matrix totals and the team's top 3 requirements (X, Y, Z), draft an ADR recommending a tool. Include: context, decision, 3 alternatives considered, key risks, migration effort estimate in tokens (not days), and a 6-month review date.

list.item: gh — Compare GitHub Projects directly via CLI (brew install gh)
linear-cli — Linear ops (issue export, automation) (https://github.com/evangodon/lr)
jira-cli — Jira issue export, scripting (brew install ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/jira-cli)
glab — GitLab Boards via CLI (brew install glab)
notion-cli — Notion content export (npm i -g notion-cli)
Jira (SaaS) — REST + Webhooks. Most powerful; slow, complex permissions.
Linear (SaaS) — GraphQL + Webhooks. Fastest UX; smallest ecosystem.
ClickUp (SaaS) — REST. Most features; risk of bloat.
GitHub Projects (SaaS) — GraphQL (Projects v2). Best when code+issues live together.
GitLab Boards (SaaS/OSS) — REST. Same, with self-host option.
Azure DevOps Boards (SaaS) — REST. Strong in regulated/Microsoft shops.
Notion (SaaS) — API. Better for docs+light tracking.
Trello (SaaS) — REST. Solo/small-team kanban.
Asana (SaaS) — REST. Marketing/cross-functional teams.
Define the top 3 must-have requirements BEFORE looking at any tool — anchors the weights.
Score from team-measured PoC data, not vendor marketing.
Pre-commit to a reversible decision: "we'll re-evaluate in 6 months, here's the rollback plan."
Cap evaluated tools at 4–5; more turns into analysis paralysis.
Always include an "incumbent + zero-change" baseline as one of the options.
Capture migration cost at item-count granularity (#issues, #automations, #integrations) before signing.

template.purpose: Per-tool evaluation scorecard: setup, daily usage, team features, reporting.
TCO calculation template: direct, indirect, hidden costs with three-year total.
Script: computes weighted totals from a YAML scorecard file.
